speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emmett Utt recalls some of the unusual happenings of the old days. Justice for strangers, for Chinese, and for ex -convicts could be far from just. Here are stories about Indians, gypsies, and bootleggers, about getting around by horse, and about getting through the Depression, about a boy showing off at a carnival game, and about farmers getting used to motorized machines. Emmett also expresses his feelings about how the IWWs function to improve laboring conditions in both logging and farming. It explodes in there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It self -destructs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well that's what it should do. After you listen to it, it just shrivels up and dies.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: How you want us to go?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Go ahead and leave that one. I got some more in the afternoon.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I'll let you take it out.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I know. I got my pipe in.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I just smoked about one or two of them a day. Got them in the morning and then after dinner I smoked one. Yeah, I'll smoke one of yours. I'll get somebody around. I want to show off to give us a little smell.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I wonder what it's been some country boy.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Everything changed so much so it's... you can hardly imagine a country changed as much as this has in one man's lifetime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's funny that you say that, Emmett, because it seems like this country has changed a lot less than most places have.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know, you could strike out from here and go clear up to Bogle and Elk River and the only clear land you'd see was like hog meadows up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how the meadows are on the side of Bogle?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And there's a few little cabins, log cabins, along the road. I can remember when you go clear from here, you go by this house here up to Old Beckrick Road. And that guy said outside there wasn't hardly a field between here and Elk River. Bogle was just sitting out there in the junglers, timber all around. When you leave Bogle and go to Elk River, you left civilization. When they run in there, it was... Well, they had an old wagon road in there but then you could get over to even walk about four horses and a wagon up and down them mountains. But the railroad was the thing that went in there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know, Elk River.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Now you go up there and you've got a highway all the way in.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, brother.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, it was a two -day trip from here to Elk River. It wasn't teeming away. The folks used to go up there and come all the way someplace along the side of Bogle. They'd cap overnight and go on in to Elk River the next day. Used to go up there fishing. It's just the magic. People go fishing. They had to do that nowadays. Takes a four -horse team and a wagon to load up all your grubbin' stuff and the blankets. Started out back up there. It was a two -day trip.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: How many people go fishing nowadays?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Boy, when you got there, you could really catch the beach.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, gee.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Unbelievable. You would not even believe it. I had my mother fish a friend that pond. I'd up her pond. She had two poles. She had me taking the fish off and re -baiting them. About that time I got her baited, she'd come up with another fish. I just kept me going. Taking the fish off and baiting her hook. Oh, my God. Take that way no more.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I've heard that a lot of the cricks, like around where I live, used to run year -round. You'd never know what to look at them now. No. They'd just rise and fall. Sure, sure. I heard you could catch fish in them all year round. I heard you'd catch fish in them all year round. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I don't know. I don't know if you've been back in the back country, other side of Clarkie, up in there. Oh, they got them cricks. One crick that I remember we broke down a few years ago. My wife's brother -in -law helped us through there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: A lover crick.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know what that is?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, anyway, you go down there and you go down the canyon, mountains on both sides of you there. It's just forest, you know. And some place down through the sun never hit the ground. And then we was up there a few years ago and they'd log that off. And there the hills was. There wasn't a hundred trees standing on the hills. If there was a tree standing, they'd knock it down. There was something. There just wasn't anything left there. And down where the crick was, you could hear the crick running. We was up there the first time. This time you could see it. And this log crisscrossed like that all the way up and trashy and hot. Boy, the fish couldn't live in that water.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Just ruined it. There ain't nothing left there. I talk about reforesting. After doing any reforesting there, I never seen no sign of it. They didn't just take the big log out and leave the little ones. They just cleaned the side of the hill.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What they call that? Clear loggin'?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Nothing left.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: We went there and never left. I think I was taking a stand up there. For miles and miles down that gun canyon.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Now there's trees standing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You see the little trees laying on the side of the hill. They knocked over. Total destruction.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you, Emmett, about I wanted to start when you were a boy and ask you what you remember Princeton being like when you were a kid.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I can go back and remember the saloons. I used to have about five or six saloons over in the town.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Money made and lost. Probably got his lumberjacks to come down there and have a roll of money on them. Probably stick in the woods all winter. They'd come out and buy guys. Like I say, Princeton was rip -roaring. One old guy that lived over here I don't want to mention no names, but anyway he rode a saloon.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And, uh, buy guys.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They'd take a guy and get him drunk. Give him free drinks. The guy would drop in and roll him. Take his roll off him and leave him laying out in the ditch. Somebody would find him or he'd lay there until he woke up. He'd be broke. Oh, it was loud that night. Well, I heard that because I didn't see it as a kid. But, uh, I had no one celebration I had over here when I was a little kid. Oh, there used to be big crowds coming to Princeton before the July celebration. They'd have prayer fields and the berry would go around and they'd throw balls at the cats. Just pinning out any things. Thought we was going to go down to my granddad's down below printing about two miles for dinner. And by gosh, we drove down the road all the way between Princeton and Hampton. The drunks were laying on the road. And my dad had to drive a team around this way to keep them running over them. And they'd say, don't you dare run over me, you young, you know. I can remember them saying that and checking something again. I don't know how they got down the road. I guess they started down the road as far as they could get to keel over and lay down. My dad was driving a horse and a spy got us around this way to keep them running the wagon over. And them guys would lay down and cuss you. Just dare you run over them. I don't know why I was one of them celebrations. I think that was after the saloons got kicked out, though.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It may not have been.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They had a concession over there you could get three baseballs for 15 cents.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, for a dime.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And you could knock three cats off while they give you a quarter. I don't know, boy. Oh, I used to throw chipmunks and everything. I'd always have a pipe full of rocks, flammin' rocks and some damn thing. I'd know it all the time. I got pretty good.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I got some.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I threw them cats. Boy, I could take three balls, knock them cats down and try to think of something to set them up. I had a few dollars in my pocket, just two bits of this through. I had to pay 10 cents out of them. I went to 15 cents exactly because the cuzby died in the throw. And I got some. The guy finally bargained for throwing. He wouldn't let me through in the morning. So, boy, I tell you, I was walking 10 feet tall. I got bargained through in there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's pretty good.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So, anyway, I happened to larder around. I come back then. The old guy was squawking around trying to get somebody to throw. And the old guy told me, Come here, kid. Show them how to do this. It won't cost you nothing to throw. Just show them how to do it. Boy, I just tried to throw it up there and I got three balls. I tried to knock them cats out of L .A. And I missed everything. And they went out. And they would knock the cat down. Boy, them cats out there. Yeah, they sure showed us how to do it. My feet were as red as that line. I got back out and they went around there again. That ruined my day. That ruined my day. I was a tall cat around kind of left. Just going to show you no ordinary. I could have got two of them anyway. I missed everyone. I was trying to hit them. I just tried too hard. You were showing off.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Don't you get me showing off?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Just don't play.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So, I don't know. There was quite a little movement happening over here. They had two stores. Libby Barn.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And I guess maybe some earlier part of my life. They had the slumes.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And the houses of Ill Repute. And the house of Ill Repute. Yeah, I guess in early days. Well, I don't know. I was pretty little.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They kicked that out a long time before they got rid of the slumes. Yeah, that went out of there. I don't know what law or what happened to that. But I can just remember that was a long time ago. When I was a little kid, kids would just go and play around there. Hide and seek around that kind of place. But it was out of operation, man. They still had slumes in there until nineteen -one, one or three.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The prohibition went in. Well, I guess they outlawed it around here before it went in in the country. And it was in the teens. I think it was about fourteen, fifteen, someplace in there. Slumes went out. I don't know if prohibition went in. Well, what I hear is that after that happened, that they were still pretty wet even though it was illegal. Everybody just started... Bootleggers.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Montana was still wet. Yeah. Well, I don't know. They used to bootlegger a lot of whiskey from Montana over in Wallace, Idaho. Kill all of them. They'd have, oh, I know my cousin. I used to go from British to them. Some guy would take them, big old duderbeggers. Duderbeggers, the president did, I think they called it, big one. They made three different size. The big one, I think they called it, was really the president. He was a big son of a gunner. They'd take an armor plate, them things, and build an armor plate right down the back of the tires so they couldn't shoot the tires. And they'd go over to Montana by a guy and shoot. And, of course, the cops would be laying for them. They used to have those Ripperorn days, boy. You know them guys running the liquor in Idaho. But then them guys all used to be big duderbeggers. The farmer played them. So you couldn't shoot through them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, gosh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like around here? Was there much trouble from the law about that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I know that up in Mobile, Pat Malone didn't give a darn. No. He just went the other way. Clarky, I don't think they ever did know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The prohibition never took effect up there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anything else, anything else law doesn't count. That was wide open.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Clarky.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it tougher down here or was it about the same?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, well, of course, after prohibition come in, they quietened down pretty much here.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But Clarky, I don't think they ever did a law. I don't think the law ever got to them. It was so open again because I don't think... But I heard by gosh, everything was wide open after that. Even by gosh, they... Women part of it, I think, by gosh, is still there yet. They don't think they ever did get rid of that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Of course, they...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They had a lot of ways of bootlegging leggings from Canada.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: My little neighbor, I was over here, Virgil Adag, you ever hear him?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He lived right over in this big white house over there. Oh, when I was a kid, he got an airplane. He went up to Spokane and took lessons up there flying and then he got it brought him a plane. And he done everything. Everything was on the crickets. He was trying to make all the money he could with it, I guess. But he got to going to Canada. And he'd bring booze down from Canada. Well, they had two places to land. One place right down there, you know, where the... Highway 95 goes north of Coeur d 'Alene. That field off to your right there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, he was watching.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And at a certain time, you'd see cars going down there and they'd line up across that field. All the headlights pointing one touch to another. And they'd make a mark. And come in after dark. He'd always come in after dark. And my guys would get the cars all lined up out across that field and he'd come down and land down between the cars. They'd all go to the booze they had ordered and take off. And all be... He'd land and then be taken off in about 15 minutes. There wouldn't be nobody there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anybody notified the cop?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I mean, he got there and they'd be all gone. I'd see them down here on this field down here. And one night I come home and make caillou. And I heard an old bird coming in. Boy, he used to come up the valley and then he'd circle right over here. The old road used to run right over his head. I was coming over there. And my guys, I don't think he was over 20 feet above my head. He'd just come out. I couldn't see him. I couldn't see him up there. Couldn't see him. No light or nothing. Boy, he just rode over the top of my head. About scared of the car used to death. And there's two trees down there on his place. That was his landmark to the skyline. He'd go down there by a guy without no lights. No light down there or nothing. He'd go down there by a guy and go out between them two trees and set it down. He was right on the runway. That two trees was his mark. He'd get up here and he'd line them trees up and then he'd go out between them trees and just set it down. Dark and black as you have. I don't know how the devil done it. He wouldn't lick a lot of whiskey in Canada. Then he went and got a bigger plane. One in Holland about 25 pounds or something. Then he got to Holland and the Chinese in Mexico. He'd get so much ahead.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I guess he... somewhere he got a hunch but they'd lay and pour him up at San Francisco I think it was. He got a hunch by a guy. After him he had a whole load of chinks in his plane. He spotted a cop actually down there and he landed the plane. He told them darn chinks to step tight and he took off. Let the plane sit in there and they did claim it. Of course they found the plane but then they couldn't prove it was his. They had a fair time over it but I guess he got out of there. They didn't know he couldn't prove it. He blew it in there. But he lost the plane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he doing with Chinese?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Old guys were paying them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. He'd haul them into this country from Mexico.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what reason?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh I suppose cheap labor. I don't know. The Chinese would pay to get in here. I don't know. Probably $100, $150 a head or something. I don't know how much on his flight he was. Maybe $500 a head. He was making good money as long as it lasted. He'd haul about 25 of them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what they'd be working on after they got here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: By gosh I don't know what the heck they'd be doing. I mean the only thing that I knew they were using them for or that they were working on was in the mines. Well mines and all those.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: See they had some over here that was natives in the warring area. But it wasn't the people in the United States paying Virgil to get them in. The Chinese were paying. They wanted to get into this country and they were against the law for coming here legally. I guess they can now. I don't know what the law is now.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But at that time the Chinese couldn't get in here at all. He wasn't bringing them all the way up here. He was bringing them to San Francisco. He was bringing them up to California.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Take them off where he could get rid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know, Emmett, about the murdering of the Chinese on Camas Creek?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Chinese?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, when they were mining. I mean we know that that's happened and I want to know what you're worried about.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, all I know is just...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Just like I was hearsay, I know that used to be up here on Gold Hill, a bunch of Chinese up there got. I guess they killed a whole bunch of them up there. I've heard figures from 50 to 150 of them. And another person up there that started this poor guy on the other side of the mountain was at Hangman Creek. That's where he got his name. I understand. There was a bunch of Chinese up there. That's where that creek got its name, Hangman Creek.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About the Chinese that got killed around here, what happened? Was anything done? Did they look for the people who had done it? Did they know who did it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't think they looked very hard. I got somebody to go through a bunch of troubles, I suppose, and I don't know. I don't think they looked too hard. No Wallace one time up there. They wouldn't let a Chinese get off the train. A bunch of them got killed up there. Wallace. And they got down to where the Chinese couldn't get into that country at all. They go in there and they mine, you know, and take their... They need rice. Nobody made any money off of them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They need rice and when they get a fortune made, why don't they take off?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They've done a lot of prospecting. You can go up on Gold Hill there. And there's just all kinds of prospecting. Gold, I look around that hill up there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They're dangerous.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I know a friend of mine had a home to the town Gold Hill. We was going up there one day and he showed me a no prospect hole. He didn't know exactly where the scene was. He didn't know exactly where it was, but it was kind of open ground there. We went out in this buck brush. A bunch of buck brush there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So he told me to be careful, but I guess where do you go?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So we went in there up close and went through the buck brush and probably there was a hole right straight down. Enough cover over it or nothing. Well, he didn't know how deep it was, but you could drop a rock down. And drop quite a little, I was plunked in here. I bet that was 200 feet deep. I don't know how long it took that rock to go down. But it seemed like it was a bit deeper than you think it was. Like animals or even a hunter could go through there. Get a doctor or something, you know, and he walked through that bunch of buck brush. Down he could go. No cover over that whatsoever.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I want to ask you about law and order and in the early days and more things that you that you heard or remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there other murders that you have heard about?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I've heard of them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: One sad thing happened. Oh, that was way back there. Twenties, I suppose. No, it was about that back then. Maybe the last part of the teens. I was just a kid. Had to be in the teens. They had a back robbery down at the coal patch. Had a big reward out for it. My granddad lived down there below. Prince and prince and potluck. So there was an old trap coming there. One place to sleep and of course go to the barn. All the old traps would go to the barn. I think they gave him something to eat that night. He went down to the barn and slept the next morning when I guess he took off. Well, instead of going up the road he went right up through the other barn lot and right across the road and then headed north right through. There was an old timber out there then. They all went and logged up and left the old timber. But anyway, he got word out there was a reward. Well, and he's a kind of stranger going to the new country. If something happened, he would give. Seemed to be the thing that it is. So they had a couple of young bucks down there. They thought they was cops and robbers so they got on the horses and went after them. And they caught him up on Gold Hill way up in the mountains up there. And the bad guys just queasily seen him and they just took him and shot him down. And of course when they went they thought he had probably had a lot of money on him at the bank then, Robert. That was all he was. He just had a few crests of bread in the pack sack. Nobody knows where he was going what he was doing and where he was going. So he was an innocent victim. And he shot him down. Well, I didn't know everybody was neighbors. He was a stranger and everything was just shut up. Never did nothing. Nobody got punished for it or nothing. But them two guys was murderers straight out. Didn't give a guy a chance to explain himself or nothing. Just a couple of punk -ass kids, you know. And they never would have come to trial or nothing. I guess they just buried him. That was it, as far as I know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah... Oh, they used to go crazy with that bank, Robert. I know another time I was living in Potlatch. I was back in my... Someone had me back in the 30s.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And I was married.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And the bank got robbed out of Colfax and so they had to call out and look away at the kind of car they were driving. Just to show you how crazy all those cops out in Potlatch... You know, the sheriff at the moment, deputized him. And there used to be an old lumberyard down below Potlatch, an old building set out there. Didn't say no more. But they put me in a window, facing down the highway there. And you see a green car coming up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Let him have it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I had a 30 -30 rifle. And I just hope South said, You see a green car coming up that road, but I got cut loose on it. That's what the bank Robert was supposed to be in, was a green car. Well, there would have been 50 green cars, but I wouldn't have shot one. Oh, hell, I'm so poor. Father might have gone down the green car, you know. But that's orders they gave me.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Can you imagine that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This wasn't when the cars first came out either. There were a lot of cars around.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. That was back in the 30s. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That was after I was married. I mentioned that in the 30s. But even that was not a crazy thing for a sheriff to tell you to do. Green car comes up that road, but I got set in the window looking right down the highway there. Green car comes up, but I got him out. I've done a crazy thing. I know a couple old couples. Don't know them. I read the paper I think it was over in Wyoming. Had the same thing happen. Had a guy stationed down on the highway. And the stricken colored car the robbers were supposed to be in. So they're sitting up front in the back, and here come that car by a guy, and she went bang, bang, and they went. Killed an old man in the old one. Yeah, killed an old man. I suppose they had orders. She was a stricken colored car.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well... Herschel Treble told me that it was easy for a guy that committed a crime to get away in the early days. He could go to Canada and there was just no...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, he didn't have much communications, you know. He tried something right in the guy's... He could go up here in the hoodoos. Because I can get an old cabin to stay there. I don't suppose there'd be anybody questioning him. He'd say he's up there prospecting. He could stay up there, blow it over, and then go on about his business. I suppose there'd be a lot of it down there. I don't see why not. I don't see why they couldn't. Probably more trips like that got away in this kitchen. Oh, the only way you get caught is witnesses, you know. Neighbors arrested him. That'd be about the only way they'd get caught. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know, the guy told me that you could tell who did it, usually. Something happened and when it was just real close community, you know, something happened, well, you could just figure on who. Who was probably the one that did it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, that works out. Some guy gets in trouble with the law and then... I know a kid I was screwing with over here. My gosh, he got in a mess. He robbed a post office over there one time. And he had to do a little time with that. But he got out and it didn't make no difference what happened. It was old Bob Dunn. Yeah, old Bob Dunn.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What the devil happened around here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: My gosh, old Bob Dunn. Probably didn't have anything to do with it, but they had a scapegoat. Anybody robbed something or something stolen, how it matter, it was old Bob.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He was finally by a guy who got such a reputation that he kicked him out of hot lights and told him never to come back. So he was a pretty good kid. I don't know, but one time I was working down at the mill. It was storming and snowing, I guess it was, and I had to put my chains on before I could get a pile of pot lights. So I pulled in the garage, Dunn, and put my chains on. Who walked in but old Bob... I guess I shouldn't say names. So he tickled his CV, you know. Wanted to know if he could ride the prison with me. I said, sure. As soon as I got these chains on, we were off and going. Pretty quickly he come to town copying everybody. I grabbed him, put him down, put him in jail.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And all he done was just come in town. Took him down, put him in jail, and I haven't seen him since. That's the last time I ever saw him. When I was talking to his brother, his brother was living in Detroit, worked back in the Dodge factory. And he was out here when his dad was sick. So he was out to see me, and I was acting him about Bob. Well, the last you heard of him, his own brother, was his sister lived in Seattle. And by the time somebody knocked on the door, she went to the door and wanted a handout. He didn't know who it was. He just knocked on the door, just one of them things. Me and houses there in Seattle had to knock on his sister's door. And she went there, and he always kind of had his head down. But she looked at him. He said, Bob, he looked at Regina, and he just turned away. That's the last time anybody ever... Even the family doesn't ever see him since then. Even the family never seen him since then. I said, okay, nice. That must have been awfully embarrassing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he had to knock on the door by the guys of his sister. She didn't get to talk to him, and then he just took off, and I seen what he'd done. As far as I've talked to the family, nobody's ever heard of him since.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Are they still alive or not? Would they have won?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, that was pretty irony. I think they were.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They had the home that was going, and I think it all turned pot like, brought all this timber back up in here. I think them home that went into pot like land. Pots matter, I know they did. They just turned the home that was back, and nobody went in and did anything about it, so it just reverted back to the government, and when the government sold the land to pot like, then home that all went into it. There used to be a lot of cabins up there, home that was...

speaker: Emmett Utt

I thought it was kind of funny. Get a hold of a cow's tail, hang on, she'll take you through hell and high water. So that's just about the size of that one. Just the things a kid is. Of course, the kids don't remember depressions. You get three meals a day. Don't bother kids. Sure bothers the parents, I guess. You know where their next day meals come from. Well, them days old farmers, you know, ain't like these crazy, goofy mess out here on the farm. Now, they had pigs, they had chickens, they had gardens, they had cows to milk, they had the meat. And they raised about all they had to go to town and get was salt and pepper and maybe a few spices. They had the lard. Even had the wool, you know. Of course, my mother never had a lot of wool.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know what carding wool is?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You got your card, you know, you get wool there and you work the back of the field and it gets all the seeds and stuff out of it. Makes it fluffy. But they used to make quilts. You know, everybody had their quilting frames and that's the only way anybody thought about going to town and buying a quilt or anything. Not like you do now. You made them. And they'd take that cotton and put it all over the top of the quilt and they'd sew it. One layer of cloth over the top of the quilt and one on the bottom. Sew it together. Sewing ties, sewing ties, and making quilts. Everybody made their own quilts that way and had their own wool to do it with. And you could get cotton or what they call cotton batten. Cotton was about so thick and it just rolled up a big road and you could get a roll of cotton batten and you'd make bedding out of that too, you know. Of course, the dresses were all gingham. Gingham dresses. I don't suppose you could go to town buying gingham to save your neck, man. Gingham. That was under clothes of flour sacks. You see, you had more kids went to school, bad guys. You know, they used to have, what do they call them, under skirts.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Slips.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I thought they had... Petty coats. Petty coats, I guess you could call them. They was all, 90 % of the girls going to school had flour sacks. Well, that was good cloth. You could wash the cloth up, you know. Sew them up, I guess. Maybe they'd, you could see with the dress off, you might have bluestem that goes across the back of it. Most of us would have this kind of blue stem pliers because they had a miller at Moscow. Old J .P. Miller, I think it was J .P. Miller, milling company. They made bluestem pliers. 90 % of the pliers around, just like it was, would come from there. I think it was bluestem or some other brand. The dress and the skirt would have the brands of pliers on it. We couldn't wash that out.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I haven't done that, though. I haven't been running many trips. You'd take so many sacks of wheat, take it down to Poulouse, they had a mill down there, a flour mill down there. And you'd trade the wheat for flour. You'd take so many sacks of wheat and bring home so many sacks of flour. And they used to call it four sacks of the barrel. You'd probably take enough wheat down there to get three barrels of flour and that would be a winter's flour, you know. Trade the wheat even, not no money. Just trade wheat for the flour. So that's when they got through way back then in these days. It didn't take much money to change hands. If you had a grocery bill down at the store, they'd go out and make wood. And haul it down, trade the, pay the bill by so much, a dollar and a half, a quarter, whatever it was they got for the wood. Trade, that's the way they'd pay the grocery bills, like little grocery bills had.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But like I say, they would be big because everybody lived right off the land, isn't it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: All you'd have to have, I guess, is just common necessities like salt and pepper.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They'd always put the fruit up, you know, I don't care. They'd get the fruit. I know my dad had a big grocery up there on the place. They had peaches by the guys there and prunes and apples. Pears, putting in all the fruit. They just, all year they just canned fruit till they'd have the celery lined up with old fruit jars. And you had your fruit and that's the way they got the flour, you know. They'd trade the wheat for it, that didn't cost no money. So you didn't have to have much money them days to get by. You'd have to buy everything. Everything was money. Just lived like us living out here. We were living in town, just buying everything out of the store. You see, 90 % of the farm was the same way. They just raised some, you'd see a farmer around here, I guess, where they really raised part of the living on the farm. I can't think of one, I'll pass. I can't think of a farmer around here, but I guess people living on the farm have got milking cows, raising pigs. Only one guy up here is raising pigs up here in the Subtercrico. I don't think there's anybody else up here in the Subtercrico. So,

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: like I say, they had the depressions. If I got people kept on eating, they didn't have much money to spend. They didn't run around in cattle access, but they'd smoke up everything. You know, it didn't bother like the depression. Now it hit the people from our gosh, it was the most helpless damn mess you ever saw. The depression hit this country. People are so darn helpless.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Be pitiful.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You want us a guy? I was hoping my guy saw I'm pulpitizing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're good. You talk me into it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I don't know. Now sir, if the depression had hit this country, just imagine. Everything inflation so high that people, well, I read the other day, come over to television, my guy said yesterday, that since things went inflation now, if you're blowing up 4400 dollar mark a year, you're proud of it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Me? I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I can't figure it out. Yeah, if you got less income than the 4100 dollar a year, you're in the private area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emmett, I wanted to ask you about the IWWs. Because I've been hearing a lot of different things about them, pro and con. I want to know what you think about what they did and what was going on.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They were one of the best things that ever happened. They got paid an hour a day around the country. It was usually to try to take the credit for that, 8 hour a day. Everything was 10 -12 hours a day. And the Wobblies, they called them Wobblies. But they got organized and of course nobody knew. You'd meet a guy on the road, you wouldn't know he was a Wobbly. You didn't have no... He could do it, they couldn't really arrest him because they didn't have no mark on him or nothing. If they could call him a Wobbly, maybe you... Many people knew who was Wobblies. Maybe bad guys just like you and I sitting here talking. Maybe if I got you to go back in them days, maybe I'm a Wobbly and you wouldn't know it. Unless I told you, because you would strip me off and I wouldn't have any credentials on me to prove I was a Wobbly. There was a lot of things blamed on the Wobblies that I'd never have happened. Now, during the time of Wobblies in this country, you'd go out here and you'd see a wheat field go up. Now whether the Wobbies done it or not, everything that happened was the Wobblies.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: IWWs.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The same had done it. I know Potlatch got so scared down here that they had logging camps up. And like I said, they even had guys set up along the right of way going into the rail tracks, going into logging camps. And bad guys would Wobbly come along there and bad guys would have rifles. Some of the guys just drawed wages for that. They would shoot them, you know, but I don't know. You never know who was shooting or who wasn't. If a guy come up to that gone rail track walking up to that camp, bad guys didn't. He didn't know who he was. They had orders ordered, I guess, to shoot him. Put him under arrest or something. I didn't know what they were supposed to do. I know my uncle went up there. He hired out to do that. He was supposed to be laying in the shade all day. He said he wasn't a shot nobody. He was a dozen of them, really. I know a friend of mine was cooking up in the cabin. He got wired to a guy, what they call a flunky, a bull cook. He had to cook him in a bull cook. This guy was a bull cooker. Anyway, this fellow found out. Well, it was Elton Tribbles, Herschel Tribbles' brother, was cooking, I think. Anyway, he said he found out some way that that guy was a wobbly in that bull cooker. The boss didn't know it. He was as good a guy as ever. He'd done good work. He was a good guy.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anyway, he didn't fire him. He found out he was a wobbly. He said, I'm too damn good to help bad guys. He wasn't going to fire me.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He had to just say, well, you and I, why are they out?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: If you're a wobbly, you're way out. They finally got an eight -hour day, by gosh. After they got what they wanted, they got camps all cleaned up. Oh, boy, the logging camps. They had the bunk houses up there, all lousy and bed budged. You had the pack you owned the bedroom in. I remember the kids, the tramps going up the road, but I got settled in the logging camps. They always had the beds all on their back. They had to take their beds all in, and when they'd come out, they'd have to pack it out. No bathing facilities, no nothing. They just lived like animals. That's what the wobbly's got that cleaned up. They'd put in, by gosh, bathing facilities, furnished the bedding, give you decent meals, an eight -hour day. So when they got things they wanted, it was just no more wobbly's. It was nothing. It was just like they were just something. It went like in the light of day, just no more. That was it, nothing, when they got what they wanted. But everything that went on around the country was blamed on the wobbly's. And I think a lot of the wood fields were set just to blame it on them. One of the things they'd do, they'd done a lot of damage done.

speaker: Emmmett Utt
words: Who done it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. You think it's phosphorous.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know what that is?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it's stuff they used to use for poison.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Mix up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But the stuff is, you know, it comes and you buy it in sticks, kind of a yellow stick about so long, about so big around, about five or six of them in the water, in the jar. Well, you'd take that out of the water and you'd throw it out in the field. Sunshine autopilot. And it just gets so hot and all at once, it just flames up and away she goes. Maybe it might take a half a day or something to get warmed up to go, but you'd sure do it. I could start a fire down here and wind down down at the barn lot. I had some sitting down in my well down there. I forgot about it. The water just dried up. One day it was down there by the guy, and she was smoke curling up there. It was right by that building. Smoke curling up and boy, got that jar by the guy that had them blowed up and had them flamed yet, but she was smoking. Well, that dug a hole in the bed. You know, you could throw a stick of that out in the wheat field or a straw pile or even in your house, baby, and if you didn't know it, you could be cleared of another state in time to finally take off. It might take two or three days, you know, depending on the weather. That stuff's got to be kept in water all the time. As long as you keep it in water, it's all right. But it would dry out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about the strike that happened here?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Now, I've been told that they did strike the camps, and that's what made the change the conditions.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yes. They struck the camps, yeah. You betcha. They tied them up. That's what they tried to do to break the strike, you know, was to put them guys up there with guns and then get strike breakers in and keep the camps going. But, oh, no, they tied things up pretty good.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Were most of the Wobblies foreigners or were they mostly...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Could be anybody. Just like you and I. As far as I remember, I was a pretty good -sized kid when there was a Wobble I happened. And you never knew who he was. Just like you and I, the neighbor across the street. Just like them damn Klu Kluxers. They was in here one time, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. You didn't know who they were. It was a secret organization. But, oh, yeah, there was Klu Kluxers in here one time. I don't know who all belonged to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do? What did they do?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: A lof of skullduggery. People by the guys say something they didn't like. Like I say, they didn't have no law damn hard in the country. Oh, like some guy by the guys trying to pull something, you know. They'd go out by the guys and warn him by burning a cross in front of his house.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: If that didn't wake him up, what was going on?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He'd get the hell out of the country by the guys and they'd ride him out on the rail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when this was? Was this about the same time as World War I?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, let me see. Klu Kluxers. I was just a kid. I imagine back in the teens or someplace when that was going on.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Just right in doing World War I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the things that happened and any of the guys that got run?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I can't recall anything that actually happened. Not around here. That's all I know. There was an organization in here, but I can't think of anybody burning a cross in here. But then around the country and back east it was terrible. Klu Kluxers, they was all over. That was a big organization one time. I can't say whether they were a good dealer or a bad dealer.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: We called it Klu Kluxers. About being called the Wobbly, but I think both of them had their good points. Kind of a vigilante outfit. You say vigilante by gosh, a sheriff. Guys run twice as fast by gosh if you heard vigilante was from a sheriff. I don't know

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but it depends. If it's going to be a reign of terror, then it might not be so good.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, the names I know I've kind of been told a little about what the organization is about. But you know by gosh, you're living here and some guy by gosh is running another man's wife, you know. Doing it on the sly. While the Klu Kluxers get a hold of it, what they warn him, by burning a cross in front of his house.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And that's usually one treatment, isn't it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That was the end of that. It's just kind of a law and order that kind of keeps things on even now. Well, somebody by gosh is around stealing things around the country. They'd serve him notice the same damn way and it's a pretty good hint to get out of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that sounds like vigilantes. And you hear about the Ku Klux Klan in the South, you think from this terrorizing the Negroes.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. Well, that was in the South. They used it there to raise heck with the Negroes. But we wouldn't have no Negroes out here.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It was just a, I don't know what the Ku Klux Klan does, KKK, what the heck is that thing?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: We just call them Klu Kluxers.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. The lights of the, I don't know, three Ks.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But the vigilante, I don't know if that was all the same things as the Klu Kluxers or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it sounds like a vigilante group is what you mean.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, it was. They was, you know, the Klu Kluxers, they'd get their hoods on, you know, they were secret, come secret, you know. You'd see a group of them out on the night, and they went on a march, they had them hoods over their faces, like a bunch of ghosts. I'd never seen any.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Seen pictures of them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But nobody knew anything about it. They had the robes and the hoods over their face. They'd go out there by the guys, burn the cross, and they'd all disappear, get their clothes off. Might be your neighbors, you don't know who the heck was doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I really shouldn't have gotten off the IW's, because I really want to know what you know about them, and what you remember as things that went on about that time between them and the law.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, all I know is, is those woods, they was on track for more wages, better living conditions, even on the farms, but only the lumber companies, the farmers, by the guys. You know, the farmers, by the guys, would just pay a man, well, a lot of them wouldn't have to pay any wages at all, you know, they'd come in and work by the guys, get their board and room and a place to sleep. Maybe they'd give them a little tobacco money. That was wages for a farmhand. Well, that wasn't good enough for a working man, I guess. They'd burn up a few woods to feel it by the guys, I guess, to kind of convince the farmers that wasn't satisfactory. I don't know just what the heck it was all about, but anyway, it changed the whole thing around the country. The farmers used to work best by the guys from daylight in the morning till dark at night. No hours, no such thing as so much an hour or nothing, just day by guys, when a day's work was daylight till dark.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The same way the cows were, they'd run ten hours a day up here in the woods. Maybe some of them were twelve hours. When the mail first was built down here, it was a twelve hour day, running two ships, that's where they started that mail up when they first built it in, I guess, 1906, they opened it up and started running. I was in that mail the first day they ever ran. Oh, yeah, it was quite a sight, you know. That was the biggest mail in the world one time. Oh, yeah, I was just a little kid. I still got a vivid idea of them cows just going back and forth, you know. That was a start up, twelve hour days, two ships, running two ships twelve hours a day, that was the start of the mail. And they finally got it down to ten hour days. And the old, the Wobblies got around to shooting with their work, I guess, it was about an eight hour day, and it's been that way ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about the 4Ls?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, that one lazy, lousy, layman, just a damn oil laborer. Just a company union, wasn't a national union, just a company union. And they were just organized to keep the national union from getting in like the air for valors. Well, that's about the only union they had, everything was air for valors. It was a national union. But they'd keep their mouths, they had their little, well they had two or three unions, one was the 4Ls, and it was still company controlled. All they done was take them back, I said, now that I owe you pay check, pay your dues, every month for a volunteer union. The company called the score. The union didn't have no power whatsoever, it was just a joke. The only thing it was, they was organized, but the big union couldn't get a foothold in it. And they didn't get a foothold in it until Roosevelt got in. Roosevelt got in, like I say, couldn't stop till that nonsense.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they had the I .E .U. down here one time, after the 4Ls played out, but then they got an order called the I .E .U., Industrial... I don't even know what the national stands for. That was just another company organization, but that was the last one in before they had to find a little boat, but they got to get the union in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to belong to the 4Ls if you worked for Potlatch? Huh? Did you have to belong to the union?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, oh yeah, that was automatic, that was automatic when you signed up for the job down there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: The dollar out of your paycheck every month, I guess, was a long time union. Bet you you had to belong to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, one of the things that some guys I've talked to, they seem to feel that the Wobblies were fine, they liked what they did, and yet they kept working when the strike was called. And they said they were just local boys, supporting their families, and they just kept working. And they liked the changes that came about. And I wondered if that was generally true.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: But the Wobblies were going to work after?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, no, the local guys that liked the Wobblies and what they were doing didn't strike and kept on working.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, they didn't strike like the Wobblies, I guess, they just kept on working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that most of the local people didn't strike and it was just the loggers that were in the camps?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I'll tell you, this strike that the Wobblies had was kind of a different damn deal than what you know as a striker nowadays. But nobody knew a Wobblie. Well, you'd go up and they'd hire a man up there in the woods. Well, the bad guys, once the bad guys would have a log die in the woods that would have a wedge or something go over the log and bang and hit the saw. Well, every time they'd hit a saw down there by the big steels or tail dog or something left in the log, it'd be about $500 for a new saw. So, they'd order like old Nixon, they had a lot of dirty tricks. Well, they just drove the damn company nuts. You know, they couldn't know who does or nothing, but just dirty tricks. Like I said, leave everything. I was sawing down there and I don't know when this was put in the log, but a big hollow log coming from me and I was running the carriage sawing. And I sawed through and started to hit the hollow part of the log and I seen it flop. And I stopped the rig and looked and when I pulled that out of there, well, it was a stack of dynamite and caps and something. But there was a Wobblies or just something like that, put it back in that log sometime by a guy and forgot about it or quit that night or something. But that log come down from me. Well, if I'd have hit that dynamite with that saw, I probably wouldn't have been here talking to you now. I don't know whether that happened. But the darn stuff was dry. It laid out there in the pond, but it was a big seeder log and it flowed high. And when I got up in that log where that damn dynamite was, I guess the water hadn't got to it. It was just as dry as a bone. I tell you, boy, when I pulled that gun sack out of there, I mean, we got to look and see what it was. I was scared to live in that kind of a thing. I sure looked all the hollow logs over after that pretty good before I sent that saw through. So I suppose maybe, I don't suppose the Wobblies done that, but it could have been in that years. Put it in there a long time ago, maybe some guys thought it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People have said to me that they don't think that the IW's did much actual sabotage in this area.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, they didn't do much. They'd just done enough and then the other people done dirty tricks that were blamed on the Wobblies. Actually, they just drove the company nuts and they couldn't pin it on anybody because like I say, they didn't have no card or anything that they could find on them. They had some, I don't know how they communicated with one another. I suppose they had a, like any lodge, I suppose they had a signal that they could make it so the other guys know who he was.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So anyway, I don't know. They were the ones that really got the eight hour day. Not better conditions in the mining camps. They wasn't only working the lumber camps, they was up in the mines. They was in all industries. All over the country. It wasn't local. All over the whole United States.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It was a United States wide organization. It wasn't just potlatch here, it was a few lumber companies all over the north western, wherever it was lumber companies. It all worked the whole thing. So they'd finally give up and went on the eight hour day and cleaned up the camps. After everything was all done, mining camps were the same way. They was a louded up mess too. Take a guy down and work him, you know. It was a heck of a mess. When they got everything all cleaned up, they had decent mugs for the guys to sleep in. Decent blankets and quilts and cleaned up the bed bugs and lice.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Cleaned them all up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Got a place where a man could take a bath.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Before they done that, they didn't even have a chance to come in out of the woods, buy a guy's shoes, soaking wet. Any place you could dry your shoes off. A lot of guys slept with their shoes on because they'd take them off. They'd swivel up. Didn't dare take their shoes off because there was no place to dry them. Didn't have enough gold or nothing to dry them by, by a guy. They had a nice old cold bunk house. Like I say, I don't know. They just had a way of harassing the companies. Different things happening. They'd just pull the dirty drinks. Just drove to the mining companies and the mill companies. Not until finally they had to give up and buy a guy's shoes and give them what they wanted. After that, no more wobbly. That's why they just disappeared. One year you'd hear wobbly tales all over the country. Who's a wobbly, who wasn't. Everybody would be somewhere on the road. You didn't know who was a wobbly or not. You didn't know who your next neighbor was. When I was away by a guy, they got what they wanted. She said, like that, they'd got it. Never heard no more about the IWW. She just disappeared. When they needed it, it was there. They all seemed to have joined up with it. Somebody, I don't know where the headquarters was or nothing. Nobody knew nothing about it. They didn't seem to have no headquarters.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know whether they paid dues or know nothing about it. It's one of the mysteries. Like a little bit of dinner come day to day with a guy and gone. When they got what they wanted, I guess that was it. They're just gone.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So, no, I'd been better off if I'd been born about fifteen years before I was. I just missed all that real go -go stuff. They had around, you know, 1890, 1995, up to 1900.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh boy. Shoot them up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Kill a guy back up.
